summary: Gruber examines Apple's full-screen ad for the F1 movie in the TV app, which linked users directly to the web to buy tickets without any warning interstitial or scare sheet. He highlights the hypocrisy: Apple requires third-party developers selling digital content to show scary warnings before linking out to the web, but exempts itself when promoting movie tickets. He explains the technical distinction between digital content and real-world purchases, but argues that this distinction is meaningless to actual users. The core argument is that Apple's position — that linking to the web is confusing for users buying subscriptions but not confusing for users buying movie tickets — is incoherent.
key_points: Apple showed a full-screen F1 movie ad in the TV app that linked directly to the web browser with no warning or confirmation dialog
Third-party developers selling digital content are required by Apple to show scare sheets before linking users to web purchases, but Apple didn't follow its own rules here
The distinction between digital content (subject to IAP) and real-world purchases (exempt from IAP) is technically valid but practically invisible to users
Gruber argues Apple cannot coherently claim web link-outs are confusing for digital purchases but perfectly fine for movie tickets
The overwhelming majority of iPhone users neither understand nor care about the digital vs. real-world purchase distinction
money_quotes: The hypocrisy isn't that Apple didn't show a full-screen scare sheet for this link-out to the web. It's that they require other developers, who are doing it to sell digital content, to show a scare sheet/confirmation.
Pay for a movie to watch on your TV at home? That's a digital purchase. Pay for a movie to watch in a theater? Not a digital purchase.
I'd bet fewer than 1 percent. (I'd also bet that fewer than 1 percent care, which is why they don't know.)

I can't see the argument that the answer is 'Yes, it's potentially confusing for many users, but only if they're trying to buy in-app content or subscriptions, but not confusing at all if they're trying to buy, say, movie tickets.'

That's weird, Apple's new full screen F1 ad in the TV app links out to the browser to compete the transaction, but for some reason doesn't include any full screen interstitials warning of the big scary web, nor a confirmation dialog that it would open in the browser? Must be an oversight.
key_insight: Apple's insistence that linking users to the web for purchases is dangerously confusing falls apart when Apple itself does exactly that — without warnings — to sell movie tickets.
